sections.summary: Document title only: 'STANDARD FOR BERRY FRUITS' (no substantive provisions in this section).
Document code heading only: 'CXS 349-2022 Adopted in 2022' (not substantive).

Defines the standard's scope as quality requirements for fresh berries after preparation and packaging, excludes berries for industrial processing, and obliges holders/sellers to conform to the standard and not market non-conforming produce.
Lists the berry species covered by the standard and states it applies to fresh berries supplied to consumers, excluding those intended for industrial processing.

Sets minimum quality requirements for all classes of berries (intact, fresh appearance, sound, clean, practically free from pests and damage, free from abnormal moisture, foreign smell/taste, temperature damage) plus maturity, transport/handling resilience and notes exceptions regarding peduncles, bloom, and pest rules.

Provides the detailed minimum requirements (intact, fresh, sound, clean, practically pest-free, free of abnormal moisture/smells/tastes and temperature damage) and requires appropriate maturity so berries are not overripe and can develop organoleptic characteristics, with notes on peduncles, bloom, and IPPC-consistent pest provisions.

Requires berries to have reached appropriate development/maturity for their species/variety/area to express organoleptic characteristics and not be overripe, and notes that loose peduncles and natural bloom are not considered foreign matter.

Classifies berries into Extra class, Class I and Class II with specific quality criteria and allowable slight defects per class, and gives species-specific notes (e.g., bilberries/blueberries, currant panicles).

Extra class requires superior quality characteristic of the variety/species, practical freedom from defects except very slight superficial ones, and includes varietal specifics for berries and currant panicles.

Class I requires good quality characteristic of the variety/species and allows very slight defects in shape, slight juice leakage, slight skin/colour defects and very slight bruising provided appearance, quality and keeping quality are not affected.

Class II covers berries that meet the minimum requirements but not higher class criteria and allows slight leakage, shape defects, skin/colour defects and slight bruising while retaining essential quality, keeping quality and presentation.
States that sizing of berries is optional but, if used, may follow existing trade practices and be indicated on the package with the size and method used.

Allows specified tolerances for quality and size at all marketing stages with provisions for re-sorting non-conforming produce per CXG 47-2003 and defines percentage tolerances for defects and decay by class plus a 10% size tolerance if sized.
Explains quality tolerances at marketing stages, permitting re-sorting to bring produce into conformity under CXG 47-2003 and details class-specific tolerances and decay allowances.
Extra class tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of berries may not meet extra class requirements but must meet Class I, including up to 1% allowance for decay, soft rot or internal breakdown.
Class I tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of berries may not meet Class I requirements but must meet Class II, including up to 2% allowance for decay, soft rot or internal breakdown.
Class II tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of berries may fail to meet Class II requirements, including up to 3% allowance for decay, soft rot or internal breakdown.
If sized, a maximum of 10% by number or weight of berries not satisfying the declared size is allowed for all classes.

Sets presentation rules requiring package uniformity (same origin/species/variety/quality/colour/size), representative visible contents, ripeness uniformity for Extra and Class I, and allows mixtures of species/varieties if uniform in quality.

Requires each package's contents to be uniform by origin/species/variety/commercial type (or for wild berries by species/quality/colour/size), representative in visible portion, and practically uniform in ripeness for Extra and Class I.

Requires berries be packed to protect them, using food-grade, clean materials that avoid damage, complying with CXC 44-1995, with containers meeting hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria and packages free of foreign matter and smell.
Specifies container requirements for berries: containers must ensure quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance for handling and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and smell.

Provides labelling requirements for consumer and non‑retail packages that supplement the General Standard (CXS 1-1985), covering product name, origin, and commercial particulars and requiring legible marking on non-retail containers.

For consumer packages, requires labelling of produce name (and optionally variety/commercial type) and country of origin (with optional district/place name), and mandates indicating each origin when mixing species/varieties from different countries.
Requires each package to be labelled with the name of the produce and permits optional labelling of the variety and/or commercial type.

Requires each package to include the country of origin (and optionally district or place name), and when mixtures of species/varieties from different origins are present, the country of origin must be indicated next to each species or variety.

Labelling of non-retail containers of berries must follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021) and each package must bear, legibly and indelibly on the same visible side, specified particulars including origin and commercial specifications as detailed in the following subsections.

Require country of origin (and optionally district or national/regional/local place name), and for mixtures of different berry species/varieties the country of origin for each must be indicated next to the relevant species/variety.

Commercial specifications to appear on non-retail packages include class; variety and/or commercial type (with 'wild' where appropriate); weight or size if sized; with net weight and crop year permitted as optional particulars.
No food additives are permitted in berry fruits.

Produce covered by this standard must comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).

It is recommended that produce be prepared and handled according to the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and that the produce comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.
